next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

By ten in the morning the bat lab smelled like rubbing alcohol, hot plastic, and the sweet black rot the mangroves breathed through every screen. Rhea's recorder sat on a folded dish towel between a tray of guano bags and a chipped mug full of Sharpies, as if careful placement could turn it into evidence. Mina had listened to the file six times already. It was still mostly prop wash, spoonbill racket, the slap of Tomas correcting the boat against roots. Then came the wrongness inside it, a submerged whine that bent upward where no engine should have gone, and after that a wet blue smear in the video frame that made the hair on her forearms lift every time.

Rhea stood at the counter with her braid half undone and a strip of freezer tape stuck to the back of one wrist. She had not slept either. It showed more in her precision than in her face. When she was tired enough, her movements got cleaner, almost ceremonial, as if she could cut mess into pieces small enough to stack.

"Play the thirty-second segment again," she said.

Mina dragged the cursor back. The laptop gave them the same bad miracle. Dark water, spoonbills shrieking overhead, Tomas saying, easy, easy, and then the brief electric turn under the surface. Not long enough. Not clean enough. Enough to make her chest go light anyway.

Rhea listened with one hand braced flat on the counter. "All right," she said. "Three separate problems."

"Only three. That's practically a grant proposal."

"Evidence integrity, access, and your name."

Mina leaned back in the plastic chair until it complained under her....
open_hooks_to_carry: The spoonbill colony reaches a sensory peak that should feel erotic, comic, and feral at once.
Mina and Rhea witness the eel clearly in the disturbed blackwater while Tomas holds the boat steady against chaos.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...nd knocked it once against his palm. "Next time," he said, and Mina looked up because the phrase itself felt like a reprieve she had not earned, "if we're going back into those cuts, I want every rotten piece of history on the table first. I don't care if it makes you look filthier. I don't care if it makes me sound stupid for being here. I want the whole damn creek, not the brochure."

Rhea said, "Before the next boat."

Tomas looked at her. "Before the next gallon."

Mina closed her hand around the hot mug until it hurt. Outside, the post-storm light was finally gathering into actual morning, turning the mangroves from one black mass into many separate black things. The camp creaked. The bucket under the roof seam rang again. Somewhere along the walkway Beto started whistling a song he never remembered all the words to, only the tune, and the tune kept losing arguments with a bent hinge.

No one reached for her. No one told her to go.

The room had changed anyway. What had been tenderness last night had not disappeared. It had thickened, taken on weight, become the kind of fact that could bruise if mishandled. Rhea stood at the sink in Tomas's shirt with her braid crooked from haste and her scientist's questions sharpened by wanting. Tomas held the county map like it was both work and warning. Mina stood between them with old cold water back in her hands, the handle of a stolen cooler biting her palms across eight years, and understood that innocence had not been what she was about to lose.

It was privacy.

And privacy, she saw now, had been the cheapest part of the lie.
state_deltas_required: Spoonbill mating frenzy, donor traffic, and the narrowing window to obtain proof
Mina, Rhea, and Tomas become eyewitnesses to the eel, which turns rumor into a shared burden.
